step.title: Step 6

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The path of 68 steps through walkable (B/b/N) cells still causes game over. This means either:
1. The player moves in 3-pixel increments (one grid cell at a time), not 1-pixel increments
2. There's a step limit and 68 steps is too many
3. The player hits obstacles even when moving through B cells (because the player is 3x3 and can't fit through narrow passages)
Let me reconsider. The player is a 3x3 block. When it moves, it moves as a whole block. If the maze passages are only 3 pixels wide, the player needs to be centered in them. The issue might be that the player's 3x3 body collides with obstacles even when the center is in a B cell.
Let me think about this differently. The maze is made of 3x3 blocks. The player is also 3x3. The player moves one block at a time (3 pixels per action). The walkable cells in the coarse grid are the B cells. But there's no path through only B cells in the coarse grid.
Wait - maybe the player doesn't move in 3-pixel increments. Maybe it moves 1 pixel at a time, but the player's 3x3 body means it can only enter a 3x3 block if ALL 9 cells of that block are walkable. This would mean the player needs to move through 3x3 blocks of B cells.
But looking at the coarse grid, the B cells are individual 3x3 blocks separated by g/W blocks. So the player can move from one B block to an adjacent B block.
Let me re-examine the coarse grid:
```
Row 5: bgWBWgWgWBB
Row 6: BBgBgBBBgBB
Row 7: BBWBWgWgWgW
Row 8: BBgBgBgBBBg
Row 9: BBWgWBWBBBW
Row 10: BBBBBBgBBBg
Row 11: WgWgWgWBWgW
Row 12: gBgBBBBBBBB
Row 13: WgWgWgWBWgW
Row 14: BBgBBBgBgBg
Row 15: WgWBBBWgWBN
```
From (5,5) which is 'b':
- (5,6) = g (blocked)
- (6,5) = B (walkable)
From (6,5):
- (6,4) = B (walkable)
- (6,6) = B (walkable)
- (7,5) = B (walkable)
- (5,5) = b (start)
So from the start, the player can go down to (6,5) and then explore. But the BFS found only 17 reachable B cells. This means the B cells don't form a connected path to the target.
Hmm, but the fine-grained BFS found a path of 68 steps. The issue is that this path goes through individual B pixels, not through 3x3 blocks of B pixels. If the player is 3x3, it can't fit through narrow 1-pixel corridors.
Wait, let me reconsider the player size. The player has 8 pixels (not 9), and its boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]. This is a 3x3 block missing one pixel. So the player occupies cells (15,15), (15,16), (15,17), (16,15), (16,16), (16,17), (17,15), (17,16), (17,17) minus one cell.
If the player moves 1 pixel at a time, then each action moves the player by 1 pixel. But the player is 3x3, so it needs all 9 cells to be free.
Actually, I think the key insight is: the player moves 3 pixels per action (one coarse grid cell). Let me verify this by trying a single action and seeing where the player ends up.
But wait, I've already tried multiple action sequences and they all result in game over. Maybe there IS a step limit?
Let me try a different approach: execute just ONE action and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me first try a single action to see how the player moves
# and what happens
action(['DOWN'])
print(f"After DOWN: game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}, board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check player position after the move
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blue = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
print(f"Player boundary after DOWN: {blue['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After DOWN: game_over=False, board_changed=True
Player boundary after DOWN: [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]
